However, the change in properties is due to the helium produced by the $^{10}\mathrm{B}(\mathfrak{n},\alpha)^{7}\mathrm{Li}$ transmutation and can be reduced by changes in chemical composition.

The Molten-Salt Reactor Experiment (MSRE) is a single region reactor that is fueled by a molten fluoride salt (65 LiF-29.1 BeF $_2$ -5 ZrF $_4$ -0.9 UF $_4$ , mole %), moderated by unclad graphite, and contained by Hastelloy N (Ni-16 Mo-7 Cr-4 Fe-0.05 C, wt %).

The details of the reactor design and construction can be found elsewhere.<sup>1</sup> We knew that the neutron environment would produce some changes in the two structural materials - graphite and Hastelloy N.
The reactor went critical on June 1, 1965.
After many small problems were solved, normal operation began in May 1966.
The results of tests on the first three groups were reported.[2-4] This report deals primarily with the results of tests on samples removed with the fourth group.

The respective history of each lot was (1) standard Hastelloy N, annealed 2 hr at $900^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and exposed to the MSRE core for 22,533 hr at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to a thermal fluence of $1.5 \times 10^{22}$ neutrons/ $\mathrm{cm}^2$ , (2) two heats of modified Hastelloy N, annealed 1 hr at $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and exposed to the MSRE core for 7244 hr at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to a thermal fluence of $5.1 \times 10^{22}$ neutrons/ $\mathrm{cm}^2$ , and (3) a single heat of modified Hastelloy N, annealed for 1 hr at $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and exposed to the MSRE cell environment of $\mathbf{N}_2 + 2$ to $5\% \mathbf{O}_2$ for 17,033 hr at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to a thermal fluence of $2.5 \times 10^{19}$ neutrons/ $\mathrm{cm}^2$ .
The core surveillance assembly<sup>5</sup> was designed by W.
Each stringer is about 62 in. long and consists of two Hastelloy N rods and a graphite section made up of various pieces that are joined by pinning and tongue-and-groove joints.
The Hastelloy N rod has periodic-reduced sections 1 l/8 in. long by 1/8 in. in diameter and can be cut into small tensile specimens after it is removed from the reactor.
The assembled stringers fit into a perforated Hastelloy N basket that is inserted into an axial position about 3.6 in. from the core center line.
There is another surveillance facility for Hastelloy N located outside the core in a vertical position about 4.5 in. from the vessel.
These specimens are exposed to the cell environment of $\mathbb{N}_2 + 2$ to $5\%$ $\mathsf{O}_2$ .
The compositions of the two heats of standard Hastelloy N are given in Table 1.
Heat 5085 was used for making the cylindrical portion of the reactor vessel and heat 5065 was used for forming the top and bottom heads.
These materials were given a mill anneal of 1 hr at $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and a final anneal of 2 hr at $900^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ at ORNL after fabrication.
Alloys 67-551 and 67-504 were small, 100-lb heats made by the Stellar Division of Union Carbide Corporation by vacuum melting.
They were finished to $\frac{1}{2}$ in. plate by working at $870^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
We cut strips $\frac{1}{2}$ in. by $\frac{1}{2}$ in. from the plates and swaged them to $\frac{1}{4}$ -in.-diam rod.
Two sections of rod were welded together to make 62-in.-long rods for fabricating the samples.
The rods were annealed for $\frac{1}{hr}$ at $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in argon and then the reduced sections were machined.
Heat 7320 was a 5000-lb melt made by the Materials Systems Division of Union Carbide Corporation.
Part of the heat was fabricated by the vendor to $\frac{5}{16}$ -in.-diam rod and was sinterless ground to obtain the needed $\frac{1}{4}$ in. stock.
The material was annealed $\frac{1}{hr}$ at $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and then the reduced sections were machined.
The surveillance rods inside the core are 62 in. long and those outside the vessel are 84 in. long.
They both are $\frac{1}{4}$ in. in diameter with reduced sections $\frac{1}{8}$ in. in diameter by $\frac{1}{18}$ in. long.
After removal from the reactor, the rods are sawed into small mechanical property specimens having a gage section $\frac{1}{8}$ in. in diameter by $\frac{1}{18}$ in. long.
